analysis:
- Top heuristics share a clear pipeline: Pareto prefilter → cheap per-point scoring (box volume or scalarizing weights) → small deterministic shortlist → greedy selection using exact HV on the shortlist → bounded local swaps. This minimizes expensive pg.hypervolume calls while keeping solutions high-quality.
- Strong variants add caching (singleton HVs), CELF-style lazy updates (timestamps), and deterministic tie‑breakers; they also scale shortlist L with k and cap local-search work to bound cost.
- Weaker variants often recompute hypervolumes excessively (full HV per candidate per iteration), lack caching or lazy evaluation, use heavy stochastic/metaheuristic search (GA/TPOSS) without focused shortlisting, or fail to exploit nondominated filtering — all causing needless runtime and noisy results.
- Implementation nitpicks observed: many list membership checks (O(n)) instead of sets, repeated recomputation of reference diffs and products, inconsistent determinism (mixed RNGs/seeds), and sometimes large temporary arrays where vectorized, incremental updates would avoid them.
- Effective tradeoffs: precompute cheap proxies (box/product or many weight scalarizations) to rank candidates; compute full HV only for a small L; cache singleton (and if L small, pairwise) HVs; use CELF to avoid recomputing stale gains; and limit swap iterations. Diversity heuristics (farthest-first or weight diversity) improve coverage without many HV calls.
experience: Focus on deterministic Pareto prefiltering, build a shortlist scaled to k via fast box/weight proxies, then run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs and timestamps; compute exact HV only on shortlist, and finish with a bounded 1-for-1 swap loop. Use sets for lookups, vectorized diffs, and precompute acos/pairwise HVs only if shortlist is tiny. This keeps quality high and runtime predictable.
current_reflection: Focus on deterministic Pareto prefiltering, build a shortlist scaled to k via fast box/weight proxies, then run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs and timestamps; compute exact HV only on shortlist, and finish with a bounded 1-for-1 swap loop. Use sets for lookups, vectorized diffs, and precompute acos/pairwise HVs only if shortlist is tiny. This keeps quality high and runtime predictable.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prefer deterministic shortlisting + a lazy-greedy core: (1) fast Pareto filter, (2) cheap vectorized scoring to pick a small candidate pool ≈ O(k)–O(k·log k), (3) CELF-style greedy using cached singleton HVs and current_hv to avoid repeated full HV recomputes, (4) bounded local swaps that only recompute HV for affected replacements. Tune shortlist size proportional to k and cache everything.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: deterministic Pareto prefiltering; shortlist ≈ c·k via fast box/weight proxies; CELF lazy greedy; cached singleton HV deltas & timestamps; bounded 1-for-1 swaps; vectorized diffs; sets for lookups.
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points, build a small shortlist proportional to k with cheap vectorized proxies, run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs and timestamps (compute exact HV only on shortlist), batch HV calls, finish with a bounded swap loop, seed randomness and always return exactly k.
- Avoid: repeated full HV recomputations, nested per-candidate Python HV loops, non-deterministic shortlisting, oversized shortlists that blow up runtime.
- Explanation: Limits exact HV work to an O(k) pool and uses cached incremental deltas and lazy updates so quality stays high and runtime remains predictable.
